items.q: Which of the following is a fundamental particle: proton, electron, or neutron?
items.correct: Only the electron — protons and neutrons are made of quarks.
items.misconception: All three (proton, electron, neutron) are fundamental particles that cannot be broken down.
items.random_wrong: Only the proton is fundamental; electrons and neutrons are composite.
items.why: The atomic model is introduced with proton/neutron/electron as building blocks, so students don't update when quarks appear in later courses.

items.q: Why does a metal spoon in hot water heat up faster than a wooden one?
items.correct: Metals have higher thermal conductivity, so heat is transferred more rapidly through them.
items.misconception: Metal heats up faster because it has a lower specific heat capacity, requiring less energy per degree.
items.random_wrong: Metal heats up faster because it has more thermal energy to begin with.
items.why: Students confuse the rate of heat transfer (conductivity) with how much energy a material can hold (heat capacity).

items.q: What does the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies indicate?
items.correct: Space itself is expanding, stretching the wavelength of light from distant galaxies as it travels.
items.misconception: Galaxies are moving through space away from us, like objects flying outward from a centre.
items.random_wrong: The galaxies are turning red because they are getting older.
items.why: Students apply the Doppler effect literally; the expansion of space is a more subtle picture they often miss.
The answer examiners want is: Toward centre. “Tangentially” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If r doubles and v stays the same, a:
items.why: It's Halves. “Doubles” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Object on 2 m string, swinging at 4 m/s. a in m/s²:
items.why: The real figure is 8. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
